0: <title>Effects of management and co-digestion on life cycle emissions and energy from anaerobic digestion </title>
Horacio A. Aguirre-Villegas, Rebecca Larson and Douglas J. Reinemann, University of Wisconsin- Madison, WI, USA

Abstract: This study applies life cycle assessment (LCA) techniques to evaluate the processes control- ing the environmental impacts of anaerobic digestion (AD) pathways on a modeled dairy farm setting representative of Wisconsin. Seven electricity-producing AD pathways are compared against a base- case (BC) pathway to understand the effect of management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes on greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, ammonia (NH3) emissions, deple- tion of fossil fuels (DFF), and nutrient balances. The pathway selected has a substantial influence on the estimates of environmental impacts. GHG emissions range from 178 to 267 kg CO2-eq GJ-1 NH3 emissions range from 11 to 18 kg GJ-1, DFF range from 176 to 327 MJ GJ-1, and N availability ranges from 7 to 15 kg GJ- of produced energy. The results of this paper show that the environmental benefits and concerns of anaerobic digestion systems can be further improved with the adoption of manage- ment practices on-farm. Injection of digestate during land application is an effective management practice to reduce NH3 emissions, which at the same time increases N availability and reduces GHG emissions and DFF given that the manufacturing of purchased fertilizers is fossil energy intensive. Co-digesting manure with corn stover and switchgrass increases energy production. Recovering heat has proven to be an easy to adopt strategy that improves all sustainability indicators without imple- menting major operational changes. Finally, 100 kg CO2-eq GJ-1, 4.2 kg NH3 GJ-1 and 566 MJ GJ are determined for GHG emissions, NH3 emissions and DFF respectively for a compressed biogas pathway. © 2015 Society of Chemical Industry and John Wiley & Sons, Ltd
Keywords: anaerobic digestion; GHG emissions; energy; ammonia emissions; life cycle assessment

3: A naerobic digestion (AD) is not a new technology. Bacteria have been producing biogas naturally for millions of years and sewage treatment plants have been applying this concept for decades. More re- cently, AD has been applied to agricultural facilities to mitigate emissions associated with climate change and

achieve energy independence. The main advantages that have positioned AD among the most promising technologies to produce sustainable energy are flex- ibility in substrate utilization, availability of equip- ment, relatively straightforward conversion process, reduction of emissions from waste streams, reduction of odor and pathogens, and potential to contribute to nutrient control strategies. Despite these advantages,

Received August 22, 2014; revised March 25, 2015; accepted March 26, 2015 Published online at Wiley Online Library (wileyonlinelibrary.com). DOI: 10.1002/ghg.1506 Supporting Information is available in the online issue at wileyonlinelibrary.com.|SCI [ ]

biogas has been largely absent from the biofuels de- bate. This is in part due to the lack of comprehensive biogas studies that quantify the benefits and sustain- ability of this versatile biofuel through its lifecycle. In addition, even though there are some general policies that include biogas as part of broader environmental and energy goals (Supplementary Material Table S1), more specific policy frameworks and regulations are needed to boost the implementation of farm-based digesters. In recent years, the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS2) of the Energy Independence and Security Act (EISA) has encouraged the production of liquid transporta- tion biofuels, specifically corn and cellulosic ethanol, which have been the center of the biofuel discussion and academic research. The production of other biofuels including biogas generated through AD has begun to receive increased attention in the last years because they can diversify the current supply of both alternative fuels for transportation and electricity production, and they do not compete with land resources for food production. AD is a naturally occurring process in which microbial organisms break down organic material in the absence of oxygen to produce biogas, which is primarily a mix of meth- ane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2). Biogas can be combusted to produce electricity or thermal energy for heating applications, upgraded to be injected into a natural gas pipeline, or compressed to be used as transportation fuel. The remaining fraction after digestion (known as digestate) can be used as fertil- izer as it maintains the nutrient contents of the initial feedstock. Livestock manure is currently the main feedstock used in agricultural AD systems. As a result, states with well-established animal agriculture production hold the largest number of agriculturally based digesters in the USA. Wisconsin (WI) is known as America's dairy land with nearly 1.3 million dairy COWS which produce approximately 4.7 million dry tons of manure annually. The volume of manure produced constitutes a major environmental concern but also an abundant biomass source for energy generation. Given the large quantities of manure generated, more than 30 digesters have been installed at dairy farms in WI, making it the leading state in digester installations in the country. 3 At the moment, electricity is the most common form of energy produced from biogas due to available conversion technologies. 4 Despite these numbers, the develop-|ment of new anaerobic digesters in the state has been relatively slow. Many biomass feedstocks other than manure have biogas production potential when added to an anaero- bic digester. Mixing feedstocks (co-digestion) is a practice that has increased in agricultural systems that use this technology in an effort to increase the ratio of biogas production to digester volume. Recent studies have shown that co-digestion of high produc- tion potential feedstocks can increase biogas yields according to the feedstock composition combined in the digester. Radloff et al. assessed the bioenergy feedstock potential in WI and concluded that corn stover is the second most underutilized biomass source in the state after manure. 8 Work has been conducted in WI to evaluate the production of bioenergy from corn stover, but primarily as a feed- stock to produce cellulosic ethanol.9 There is a natural coupling that exists between dairy manure and corn stover as co-digestates given that both resources are generally available on WI dairy farms 10,11 Similarly, dedicated energy grasses have been considered as bioenergy feedstocks due to the reduced intensity of the cropping system, the ability to grow on less productive lands, and their increased energy ratios when compared to other biomass crops. As a result, there is potential to use these feedstocks for AD in the short and long term. European countries have been leading the imple- mentation and evaluation of AD systems and their related environmental impacts. 12-14 Over the past decade, natural gas prices in the USA have been lower than those of international markets which contributed to the slow implementation of AD systems in this country. These low prices are attributed mainly to the development of extraction technologies such as hydraulic fracturing, which allowed companies to reach vast shale gas reserves. Nowadays, the price of natural gas in the USA is less than half of that in Europe and less than one third of that in Asian countries such as Japan. 15 Projections show that even though the prices for natural gas in both the USA and Europe will be stable in the coming years, prices in Europe will continue to nearly double prices in the USA. 16 In addition, price incentives for biogas produc- tion in Europe have justified the adoption of strategies that make these AD systems fundamentally different from those in the USA. First, feedstocks in Europe can be transported long distances to large centralized digesters while still maintaining a profitable system

due to the economic incentives. Given the smaller scale of most agricultural digesters in the USA, feedstocks are limited to those which are primarily available on-farm. Second, food crops and dedicated energy crops in Europe are grown to be used solely as AD feedstocks due to their high energy densities. In the USA, agricultural-based digesters are primarily fed with manure and other waste streams that have low energy densities. Finally, on-farm management practices (e.g. digestate processing and disposal methods) differ. It is important to account for these practices when evaluating the environmental impacts of an agricultural system as they can have a signifi- cant influence on results. For example, if AD is incorporated in the manure management system it increases the ammoniacal nitrogen (N) content in digestate through mineralization. In this form N is more available to crops but is also volatilized more easily. If digestate is not injected or incorporated, N availability can be reduced significantly through volatilization losses. 18 There are a limited number of studies assessing the lifecycle environmental impacts of biogas production, and even fewer studies looking at the impacts of different co-digestion and management practices in the USA. This study aims at addressing these knowl- edge gaps by applying LCA techniques to quantify and compare greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, ammonia (NH3) emissions, depletion of fossil fuels (DFF), and nutrient availability of different AD pathways. The analyzed pathways consider different manure management practices, co-digestion strate- gies, and energy conversion processes in order to pinpoint approaches that maximize energy produc- tion and minimize environmental impacts. Results from this assessment could guide the development of regulations specific to biogas and accelerate the implementation of farm-based digesters by providing useful information about their operation and impacts. Relation to existing literature Evaluating the benefits of AD systems has been of interest to researchers and scientists for decades. Studies have primarily targeted energy potential and biogas production focusing on specific aspects of the digester design and feedstock(s) performance. For example, Gebremedhin et al. developed a heat transfer model to predict energy requirements to operate a plug-flow digester.19 Wu found that methane yield|remained unchanged with increasing mixing power in complete-mix digesters, but that energy output increased with heat additions in plug-flow digesters. Comino et al. investigated biogas yield from co-di- gesting cattle manure with cheese whey and found that up to 65% whey can be co-digested. 6 Appels et al. reviewed several feedstocks used in AD and discussed their potential and challenges. 13 The authors con- cluded that animal manure is one of the most suitable feedstocks for biogas production because of its N content, degradability, buffering capacity, and low cost availability. The authors also concluded that cellulosic sources, such as crop residues, dedicated crops, and grasses can be degraded to up to 80% in AD, repre- senting potential feedstocks for this conversion technology. These studies constitute valuable sources of informa- tion but their scope is limited for the design and implementation of biofuels' policies and to analyze operational trade-offs. Pimentel was one of the first to suggest that a lifecycle approach should be considered when evaluating a biofuel given that the production of biomass feedstocks is energy and resource intensive and can affect the net energy and environmental performance. 21 This approach was further strength- ened with the establishment of the RFS2, which defined life cycle assessment (LCA) as the methodol- ogy to estimate the environmental impacts and energy balance of biofuels. Cherubini outlined key steps in defining lifecycle GHG balances of bioenergy systems that produce electricity, heat, and transporta- tion biofuels from residues or crops. 22 These steps outline processes to include in the evaluation such as biomass source, cultivation and harvest, production and application of fertilizer, pre-treatment and transport, conversion, distribution, and combustion. The author suggests that when a crop residue is used for energy production, the auxiliary energy and material inputs of the agricultural phase can be assumed to be zero as it has no value for the system. Biogas LCA studies are relatively new and many of them have focused on existing municipal wastewater and water treatment plants. With the development of more efficient and specialized digesters, the incor- poration of agriculture based feedstocks has received increased attention within the biogas LCA litera- ture. 25,26 In spite of this, most studies available are based on defined pathways or centered on specific aspects of the biogas lifecycle. Jury et al. focused on the final use of biogas and estimated reduced

emissions in comparison to natural gas when upgrad- ing and injecting biogas into natural gas pipelines. 25 Patterson et al. quantified the environmental impacts of assembling and installing central and distributed infrastructures to produce and use biogas. 27 De Vries et al. evaluated the environmental impacts related to a new manure processing technology that concentrated nutrients in the liquid fraction and generated biogas from the solid fraction.28 Han et al. compared life- cycle GHG emissions and energy use of methane production from animal manure with emissions and energy use of natural gas and petroleum-based fuels production. Different scenarios were characterized in the Greenhouse gases, Regulated, Emissions and Energy use in Transportation (GREET) model as methane was modeled as a transportation fuel. The authors concluded that methane production has lower GHG emissions than fossil fuels production, but also has higher energy requirements.

Fewer studies within the biogas LCA literature have considered the environmental impacts of multiple production pathways and uses. Borjesson and Ber- glund's study was one of the first that analyzed different scenarios for feedstock production and energy generation. 30 The paper estimated fuel-cycle emissions in Sweden and made emphasis on how the system responded to individual feedstocks, digester scales, and transportation distances. The authors found that operating the biogas plant demands more than one third of energy consumption and that AD systems are energy positive for short distance trans- port of raw materials. More recently, Poeschl et al. described lifecycle inventory (LCI) and lifecycle impact assessment (LCIA) of 18 impact categories of single-feedstock digestion and co-digestion systems in small and large biogas plants in Germany.14 14,31 The authors concluded that the most efficient substrates in terms of environmental impacts are straw and corn silage within single-feedstock scenarios and combin- ing municipal solid waste with agricultural and food industry residues within co-digestion scenarios.

posed to developing case specific factors. Finally, there has been no investigation on the effect of manure management practices and technology choices within the biogas LCA literature. As Appels et al. argue, there is a need to further extend existing AD models since operational and management decisions are currently done by a general rule of thumb. 13 Manure management practices, such as the use of digested solids as bedding, or technology choices, such as the separation technology used, could lead to important changes in the system that result in differentiated environmental impacts. For example, using digestated solids for bedding avoids the production of other bedding materials (e.g. sand) and the need of their separation from manure prior to entering the digester. There is a need to conduct comprehensive assessments on AD systems considering environmental sustain- ability indicators and processing technologies accord- ing to local conditions and practices in the USA.

The situation and set-up of the AD industry in the USA calls for a different definition of AD pathways than the ones applied in the studies mentioned above. Corn silage and other crops are the base feedstocks of those studies; whereas in the USA dairy manure is a primary base feedstock in agricultural digesters. Moreover, the approach used in the lifecycle inventory stage by the studies mentioned above is based on average practices and emission factors (EF) as op-

Many biomass feedstocks other than manure have biogas production potential when added to an anaero- bic digester. Mixing feedstocks (co-digestion) is a practice that has increased in agricultural systems that use this technology in an effort to increase the ratio of biogas production to digester volume. Recent studies have shown that co-digestion of high produc- tion potential feedstocks can increase biogas yields according to the feedstock composition combined in the digester. Radloff et al. assessed the bioenergy feedstock potential in WI and concluded that corn stover is the second most underutilized biomass source in the state after manure. 8 Work has been conducted in WI to evaluate the production of bioenergy from corn stover, but primarily as a feed- stock to produce cellulosic ethanol.9 There is a natural coupling that exists between dairy manure and corn stover as co-digestates given that both resources are generally available on WI dairy farms 10,11 Similarly, dedicated energy grasses have been considered as bioenergy feedstocks due to the reduced intensity of the cropping system, the ability to grow on less productive lands, and their increased energy ratios when compared to other biomass crops. As a result, there is potential to use these feedstocks for AD in the short and long term. European countries have been leading the imple- mentation and evaluation of AD systems and their related environmental impacts. 12-14 Over the past decade, natural gas prices in the USA have been lower than those of international markets which contributed to the slow implementation of AD systems in this country. These low prices are attributed mainly to the development of extraction technologies such as hydraulic fracturing, which allowed companies to reach vast shale gas reserves. Nowadays, the price of natural gas in the USA is less than half of that in Europe and less than one third of that in Asian countries such as Japan. 15 Projections show that even though the prices for natural gas in both the USA and Europe will be stable in the coming years, prices in Europe will continue to nearly double prices in the USA. 16 In addition, price incentives for biogas produc- tion in Europe have justified the adoption of strategies that make these AD systems fundamentally different from those in the USA. First, feedstocks in Europe can be transported long distances to large centralized digesters while still maintaining a profitable system

due to the economic incentives. Given the smaller scale of most agricultural digesters in the USA, feedstocks are limited to those which are primarily available on-farm. Second, food crops and dedicated energy crops in Europe are grown to be used solely as AD feedstocks due to their high energy densities. In the USA, agricultural-based digesters are primarily fed with manure and other waste streams that have low energy densities. Finally, on-farm management practices (e.g. digestate processing and disposal methods) differ. It is important to account for these practices when evaluating the environmental impacts of an agricultural system as they can have a signifi- cant influence on results. For example, if AD is incorporated in the manure management system it increases the ammoniacal nitrogen (N) content in digestate through mineralization. In this form N is more available to crops but is also volatilized more easily. If digestate is not injected or incorporated, N availability can be reduced significantly through volatilization losses. 18 There are a limited number of studies assessing the lifecycle environmental impacts of biogas production, and even fewer studies looking at the impacts of different co-digestion and management practices in the USA. This study aims at addressing these knowl- edge gaps by applying LCA techniques to quantify and compare greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, ammonia (NH3) emissions, depletion of fossil fuels (DFF), and nutrient availability of different AD pathways. The analyzed pathways consider different manure management practices, co-digestion strate- gies, and energy conversion processes in order to pinpoint approaches that maximize energy produc- tion and minimize environmental impacts. Results from this assessment could guide the development of regulations specific to biogas and accelerate the implementation of farm-based digesters by providing useful information about their operation and impacts. Relation to existing literature Evaluating the benefits of AD systems has been of interest to researchers and scientists for decades. Studies have primarily targeted energy potential and biogas production focusing on specific aspects of the digester design and feedstock(s) performance. For example, Gebremedhin et al. developed a heat transfer model to predict energy requirements to operate a plug-flow digester.19 Wu found that methane yield|remained unchanged with increasing mixing power in complete-mix digesters, but that energy output increased with heat additions in plug-flow digesters. Comino et al. investigated biogas yield from co-di- gesting cattle manure with cheese whey and found that up to 65% whey can be co-digested. 6 Appels et al. reviewed several feedstocks used in AD and discussed their potential and challenges. 13 The authors con- cluded that animal manure is one of the most suitable feedstocks for biogas production because of its N content, degradability, buffering capacity, and low cost availability. The authors also concluded that cellulosic sources, such as crop residues, dedicated crops, and grasses can be degraded to up to 80% in AD, repre- senting potential feedstocks for this conversion technology. These studies constitute valuable sources of informa- tion but their scope is limited for the design and implementation of biofuels' policies and to analyze operational trade-offs. Pimentel was one of the first to suggest that a lifecycle approach should be considered when evaluating a biofuel given that the production of biomass feedstocks is energy and resource intensive and can affect the net energy and environmental performance. 21 This approach was further strength- ened with the establishment of the RFS2, which defined life cycle assessment (LCA) as the methodol- ogy to estimate the environmental impacts and energy balance of biofuels. Cherubini outlined key steps in defining lifecycle GHG balances of bioenergy systems that produce electricity, heat, and transporta- tion biofuels from residues or crops. 22 These steps outline processes to include in the evaluation such as biomass source, cultivation and harvest, production and application of fertilizer, pre-treatment and transport, conversion, distribution, and combustion. The author suggests that when a crop residue is used for energy production, the auxiliary energy and material inputs of the agricultural phase can be assumed to be zero as it has no value for the system. Biogas LCA studies are relatively new and many of them have focused on existing municipal wastewater and water treatment plants. With the development of more efficient and specialized digesters, the incor- poration of agriculture based feedstocks has received increased attention within the biogas LCA litera- ture. 25,26 In spite of this, most studies available are based on defined pathways or centered on specific aspects of the biogas lifecycle. Jury et al. focused on the final use of biogas and estimated reduced

emissions in comparison to natural gas when upgrad- ing and injecting biogas into natural gas pipelines. 25 Patterson et al. quantified the environmental impacts of assembling and installing central and distributed infrastructures to produce and use biogas. 27 De Vries et al. evaluated the environmental impacts related to a new manure processing technology that concentrated nutrients in the liquid fraction and generated biogas from the solid fraction.28 Han et al. compared life- cycle GHG emissions and energy use of methane production from animal manure with emissions and energy use of natural gas and petroleum-based fuels production. Different scenarios were characterized in the Greenhouse gases, Regulated, Emissions and Energy use in Transportation (GREET) model as methane was modeled as a transportation fuel. The authors concluded that methane production has lower GHG emissions than fossil fuels production, but also has higher energy requirements.

Fewer studies within the biogas LCA literature have considered the environmental impacts of multiple production pathways and uses. Borjesson and Ber- glund's study was one of the first that analyzed different scenarios for feedstock production and energy generation. 30 The paper estimated fuel-cycle emissions in Sweden and made emphasis on how the system responded to individual feedstocks, digester scales, and transportation distances. The authors found that operating the biogas plant demands more than one third of energy consumption and that AD systems are energy positive for short distance trans- port of raw materials. More recently, Poeschl et al. described lifecycle inventory (LCI) and lifecycle impact assessment (LCIA) of 18 impact categories of single-feedstock digestion and co-digestion systems in small and large biogas plants in Germany.14 14,31 The authors concluded that the most efficient substrates in terms of environmental impacts are straw and corn silage within single-feedstock scenarios and combin- ing municipal solid waste with agricultural and food industry residues within co-digestion scenarios.

posed to developing case specific factors. Finally, there has been no investigation on the effect of manure management practices and technology choices within the biogas LCA literature. As Appels et al. argue, there is a need to further extend existing AD models since operational and management decisions are currently done by a general rule of thumb. 13 Manure management practices, such as the use of digested solids as bedding, or technology choices, such as the separation technology used, could lead to important changes in the system that result in differentiated environmental impacts. For example, using digestated solids for bedding avoids the production of other bedding materials (e.g. sand) and the need of their separation from manure prior to entering the digester. There is a need to conduct comprehensive assessments on AD systems considering environmental sustain- ability indicators and processing technologies accord- ing to local conditions and practices in the USA.

The situation and set-up of the AD industry in the USA calls for a different definition of AD pathways than the ones applied in the studies mentioned above. Corn silage and other crops are the base feedstocks of those studies; whereas in the USA dairy manure is a primary base feedstock in agricultural digesters. Moreover, the approach used in the lifecycle inventory stage by the studies mentioned above is based on average practices and emission factors (EF) as op-
6: A naerobic digestion (AD) is not a new technology. Bacteria have been producing biogas naturally for millions of years and sewage treatment plants have been applying this concept for decades. More re- cently, AD has been applied to agricultural facilities to mitigate emissions associated with climate change and

achieve energy independence. The main advantages that have positioned AD among the most promising technologies to produce sustainable energy are flex- ibility in substrate utilization, availability of equip- ment, relatively straightforward conversion process, reduction of emissions from waste streams, reduction of odor and pathogens, and potential to contribute to nutrient control strategies. Despite these advantages,

biogas has been largely absent from the biofuels de- bate. This is in part due to the lack of comprehensive biogas studies that quantify the benefits and sustain- ability of this versatile biofuel through its lifecycle. In addition, even though there are some general policies that include biogas as part of broader environmental and energy goals (Supplementary Material Table S1), more specific policy frameworks and regulations are needed to boost the implementation of farm-based digesters. In recent years, the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS2) of the Energy Independence and Security Act (EISA) has encouraged the production of liquid transporta- tion biofuels, specifically corn and cellulosic ethanol, which have been the center of the biofuel discussion and academic research. As Appels et al. argue, there is a need to further extend existing AD models since operational and management decisions are currently done by a general rule of thumb. 13 Manure management practices, such as the use of digested solids as bedding, or technology choices, such as the separation technology used, could lead to important changes in the system that result in differentiated environmental impacts. For example, using digestated solids for bedding avoids the production of other bedding materials (e.g. sand) and the need of their separation from manure prior to entering the digester. There is a need to conduct comprehensive assessments on AD systems considering environmental sustain- ability indicators and processing technologies accord- ing to local conditions and practices in the USA.

The situation and set-up of the AD industry in the USA calls for a different definition of AD pathways than the ones applied in the studies mentioned above. Corn silage and other crops are the base feedstocks of those studies; whereas in the USA dairy manure is a primary base feedstock in agricultural digesters. Moreover, the approach used in the lifecycle inventory stage by the studies mentioned above is based on average practices and emission factors (EF) as op-

1: Process-based and LCA methods are applied to evaluate GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, DFF, and nutrient availability. These sustainability indicators are evaluated through each step of AD pathways in a modeled dairy farm system representative of WI. All pathways are modeled with GaBi 5 (a LCA software) for a dairy farm with 1,000 lactating cows, 605 growing heifers (<21 months), and 286 mature heifers and dry cows. 32 The size of the farm has been defined to represent a large dairy which can more feasibly operate a commercially available manure digester. This is the case in WI, where the average dairy size of farms holding digesters is approximately 2,000 animals4 As a result, the more than 200 Concentrated Animal Feeding Operations (CAFOs) in WI represent the greatest potential for anaerobic digestion in the short term.

AD pathways include the following unit processes: feedstock production, collection, and pre-treatment; biogas production, conditioning, and energy genera- tion; digestate solid-liquid separation (SLS); storage of liquid and solid streams; and land application of liquid and solid streams. Pathways are defined based on management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion process. Figure 1 shows the system boundaries that encompass all unit-processes of the analyzed pathways starting with the production of

Figure 1. Process flow and system boundaries according to the unit-processes and boundaries analyzed in this study. Dashed boxes represent variations according to feedstock production, management practices, and biogas use. Ovals represent final products. Rhombuses represent decision points that lead to different biogas pathways. Arrows represent material flows.

raw materials and ending at the farm gate with biogas processed into its final energy form. The main prod- uct is electricity (currently the most common use for biogas in dairy systems) or compressed gas depending on the AD pathway. Since the main purpose of this system is energy production and biogas is generated on a daily basis, all calculations are averaged for one day. Animal husbandry and cultivation processes for cow feeding are not included in this analysis. Embed- ded and cumulative energy, NH3 and GHG emissions associated with the production of material and energy inputs (i.e., diesel and electricity) are included in the

system boundaries; however, the production of capital goods (i.e., machinery and buildings) is excluded as its contribution to the measured outputs is marginal. 33 The functional unit (FU) for this analysis is defined as 1 GJ of produced energy. This FU reflects the main function of the system, energy production, and allows for comparisons with other fossil and renewable energy LCA studies. No allocation of co-products is applied given that the main objective of the paper is to compare changes in environmental impacts among AD pathways. An allocation method would be needed in order to assign the environmental indicators
among the co-products electricity and digestate solids and liquids. Allocation methods for this effect are explained in Aguirre-Villegas et al.

This analysis considers changes of material and energy inputs when comparing multiple AD pathways to a base-case scenario. Additions or reductions of material inputs take place with synthetic fertilizers when there are changes to nutrient availability and sand when solid digestate is used as the bedding material. Urea, diammonium phosphate, and potas- sium chloride are the purchased sources of N, phospho- rus (P), and potassium (K) to supplement crop produc- tion when needed. Availability of nutrients in digestate reaching the land is 80% for P and K, 100% for ammo- niacal N, and 43% for organic N (25% the first year, 12% the second year, and 6% the third year). 34

6: NH3 emissions, N, P, and K are reported in kilograms. GHG emissions are characterized for a 100-year time horizon and measured in kilograms of carbon dioxide equivalents (kg CO2-eq). Characterization factors used for gases other than CO2 are 298 kg CO2-eq for N2O and 25 kg CO2-eq for CH4 based on the CML 2001 method, which is an impact assessment method used in quantitative modeling that groups results in mid- point categories according to common mechanisms (e.g. climate change). 35 N2O emissions resulting from NH3 emissions and leaching are estimated using the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) definition that 0.01 of volatilized N as NH3 is converted to N2O-N emissions and 0.0075 of leached N is con- verted to N2O-N emissions. Emissions from biomass are considered biotic emissions (CO2(b), N2O(b): CH4(b)) and are evaluated separately from fossil fuel emissions (CO2(f), N2O(f), CH4(f)) to account for the CO2(b) recycling process that takes place during plant growth as it is assumed that the carbon contained in biomass has been previously captured as CO2.

DFF is defined as the energy consumed at the site plus the energy consumed in the production and delivery of that energy product. The fossil energy ratio (FER) and the energy return on investment (EROI) ratio can be used to determine the efficiency of a sustainable energy production. If the FER is 1, the amount of usable energy is less than the fossil energy expended to obtain that energy. If the EROI ratio is 1, the amount of usable energy is less than the total energy expended to obtain that energy (Eqns (1) and (2)).

8: <header>Inventory data and description of AD pathways and unit-processes </header>

Life cycle inventory (LCI) is developed using a pro- cess-based approach to capture material, energy, and emission flows for each unit-process of the AD pathways. These flows are related to system param- eters and local conditions of WI to maximize the representativeness of the model. Year-to-year tem- perature variation is captured by averaging daily data from five weather stations in each of the regions of WI from 2007 to 2011. The electricity matrix accounts for the mix of fuels that are representative of the local electric grid. 37 The necessary data for constructing the LCI includes various sources. First, data required to calculate material, energy, and emission inputs and outputs for manure AD are based on Aguirre-Villegas et al. 18 Second, switchgrass data inputs were compiled from current literature values as switchgrass is not currently produced on a commercial scale in WI. 9,38,39 Finally, verifiable databases have been used for material and energy inputs, including the National Renewable Energy Laboratory US LCI dataset, PE International Professional database, and EcoInvent. 32,40

The base-case pathway (BC), to which other path- ways are compared, represents common and basic processes including manure as the only feedstock to produce on-farm electricity with no heat recovery. In the BC, the bedding is sand, manure is collected with a diesel skid steer, digestate is separated after diges- tion using a screw press, and the separated products are stored and land-applied using surface broadcast methods. Cow diet composition, manure excretion and characteristics, and biogas production rates of the BC are described in Aguirre-Villegas et al.18 A total of seven AD pathways producing electricity are com- pared against the BC and a pathway producing compressed biogas is analyzed to provide some insight about the environmental trade-offs of using biogas as a transportation fuel. Each pathway is defined by varying one unit-process of the BC while maintaining the rest constant (Table 1). Attributional lifecycle
Table 1. Summary of the anaerobic digestion (AD) pathways analyzed.

Pathway|Feedstock|Management practices|Energy conversion Base-case (BC)|Single digestion: manure|Skid steer, sand bedding, screw press, and surface broadcast|Electricity, no heat recovery Alley scraper (AS)|Single digestion: manure|Alley scraper, sand bedding, screw press, and surface broadcast|Electricity, no heat recovery Centrifuge (CTF)|Single digestion: manure|Skid steer, sand bedding, centrifuge, and surface broadcast|Electricity, no heat recovery Injection (INJ)|Single digestion: manure|Skid steer, sand bedding, screw press, and manure injection|Electricity, no heat recovery Bedding (BED)|Single digestion: manure|Skid steer, digested solids bedding, screw press, and surface broadcast|Electricity, no heat recovery Corn Stover (CS)|Co-digestion: manure + corn stover|Skid steer, sand bedding, screw press, and surface broadcast|Electricity, no heat recovery Switchgrass (SG)|Co-digestion: manure + switchgrass|Skid steer, sand bedding, screw press, and surface broadcast|Electricity, no heat recovery Combined heat and power (CHP)|Single digestion: manure|Skid steer, sand bedding, screw press, and surface broadcast|Electricity, heat recovery Compressed biogas (CBG)|Single digestion: manure|Skid steer, sand bedding, screw press, and surface broadcast|Compressed biogas
concepts are used to evaluate each individual pathway and consequential lifecycle concepts are used to compare the marginal changes between the BC and the remaining pathways.

11: Four AD pathways are compared against the BC pathway based on changes to management practices during manure collection, digestate SLS, digestate land application, and digested solids use. The first AD pathway (AS) compared to the BC considers collecting manure with an electric alley scraper that requires 1.38 kWh ton-1 manure based on a survey conducted by Aguirre-Villegas et al. on 178 dairy farms in WI. 18 Even though the frequency of collection is increased with the alley scraper when compared to the skid steer, it is assumed that GHG emission factors from the barn are not altered by the collection method. Moreira and Satter evaluated the effect of scraping frequency on NH3 emissions, and concluded that the frequency of scraping had little or no effect on NH3 emissions from the barn floor. 41

The second AD pathway (CTF) considers digestate SLS by centrifugation which requires 3.1 kWh ton-1 of digestate. 42 It is assumed that no biotic emissions occur during the separation process itself due to the

short retention time. Separation efficiencies and characteristics of the solid and liquid streams after SLS are presented in Table S2. The third AD pathway (INJ) considers land-application of both solid and liquid digestate fractions by injection. Even though injecting manure solids is not commonly done in farms across the USA, equipment is being developed to accomplish this practice. Diesel consumption for injection is calculated as 0.69 kg ton digestate based on Hadrich et al. and Rotz et 43,44 N2O(b)-N is emitted at a rate of 1.76% of applied NH3-N after ammonia emissions for the liquid fraction and 1.97% for the solid fraction since it assumed that solid digestate behaves as untreated solid manure. 45 Several studies found that there are no changes in CH4(b) emissions with changes in application method from injection to surface broadcast; therefore, the same EF is assumed for both practices.46 In the fourth AD pathway (BED), digested solids are used as cow bedding instead of being applied to the soil, which replaces sand as the bedding material. According to the survey conducted by Aguirre-Villegas et al., digested solids are commonly replaced three times a week at an average rate of 5.5 kg cow-1 day-1 in WI dairy farms that use this type of bedding. 18 This quantity represents nearly 75% of the daily separated solids.
12: Four AD pathways are compared against the BC pathway based on changes to management practices during manure collection, digestate SLS, digestate land application, and digested solids use. The first AD pathway (AS) compared to the BC considers collecting manure with an electric alley scraper that requires 1.38 kWh ton-1 manure based on a survey conducted by Aguirre-Villegas et al. on 178 dairy farms in WI. 18 Even though the frequency of collection is increased with the alley scraper when compared to the skid steer, it is assumed that GHG emission factors from the barn are not altered by the collection method. Moreira and Satter evaluated the effect of scraping frequency on NH3 emissions, and concluded that the frequency of scraping had little or no effect on NH3 emissions from the barn floor. 41

The second AD pathway (CTF) considers digestate SLS by centrifugation which requires 3.1 kWh ton-1 of digestate. 42 It is assumed that no biotic emissions occur during the separation process itself due to the

short retention time. Separation efficiencies and characteristics of the solid and liquid streams after SLS are presented in Table S2. The third AD pathway (INJ) considers land-application of both solid and liquid digestate fractions by injection. Even though injecting manure solids is not commonly done in farms across the USA, equipment is being developed to accomplish this practice. Diesel consumption for injection is calculated as 0.69 kg ton digestate based on Hadrich et al. and Rotz et 43,44 N2O(b)-N is emitted at a rate of 1.76% of applied NH3-N after ammonia emissions for the liquid fraction and 1.97% for the solid fraction since it assumed that solid digestate behaves as untreated solid manure. 45 Several studies found that there are no changes in CH4(b) emissions with changes in application method from injection to surface broadcast; therefore, the same EF is assumed for both practices.46 In the fourth AD pathway (BED), digested solids are used as cow bedding instead of being applied to the soil, which replaces sand as the bedding material. According to the survey conducted by Aguirre-Villegas et al., digested solids are commonly replaced three times a week at an average rate of 5.5 kg cow-1 day-1 in WI dairy farms that use this type of bedding. 18 This quantity represents nearly 75% of the daily separated solids.
13: Two co-digestion systems are compared against the manure digestion system described in the BC. A flexible approach when feeding the digester is adopted to increase the volume of the AD system according to feedstock availability. The amount of feedstock that can be added to the digester is limited by two factors: (i) yields achieved on the farm, and (ii) total solids (TS) content of the additional feedstock. TS is a limiting factor since 15% TS or less is required in order to maintain a liquid AD process. 50

The first co-digestion pathway (CS) considers mixing manure with corn stover, which is removed as opposed to the BC where it remains on the field. Stover is harvested on an annual basis from continu- ous corn plantings where the grain is used for feed. A total of 321 hectares of corn are needed to supply the corn grain included in the dairy ration and a conser- vative corn stover removal rate of 3,400 kg ha-Superscript(1) is applied based on Sinistore who conducted an LCA of ethanol production from corn stover in WI.9 This number is consistent with James et al., who recom- mend removal rates remain under 38% to maintain soil fertility.51 Harvesting and baling of corn stover are conducted on an annual basis and require 6.6 kg of diesel ton-1.9 Stover is ground before entering the digester to increase bacteria accessibility to volatile solids (VS), which requires 14.9 kWh of electricity per ton of material. The volume of the digester requires an additional 570 m³ to process the extra feedstock coming from corn stover on top of the existing 4,840 m³ required to process dairy manure under the assumptions of a 28-day hydraulic retention time (HRT) and a stover density of 147 kg per cubic meter. 52 Corn stover estimates of TS, VS and methane potential are selected by averaging the results of nine studies available in the literature (Table 2). The addition of corn stover increase the TS content of the digester mixture to 9%, which is lower than the 15% needed for liquid digestion. It is assumed that N, P, and K are removed at rates of 0.9, 13.3, and 7.5 kg ton-1, which are replaced either by digestate or purchased fertilizers.53

The second co-digestion pathway (SG) considers mixing manure with switchgrass. A recent study by Ventura et al., estimated that there are 635,000 hectares suitable for energy grass production in WI.54 Switchgrass is produced under the assumption that there is readily available land on the dairy farm. 17: This unit-process targets the transformation of biogas into its final energy form. The BC considers the production of electricity with an engine-generator-set with no heat recovery, given that the majority of AD systems installed in WI adopt this approach. 4 Two AD pathways are analyzed based on variations to this energy conversion unit-process. The first of these pathways (CHP) considers combined heat and power at 35% electricity efficiency and 50% thermal effi- ciency. CHP differentiates from BC by providing heat energy instead of combusting a fraction of the produced biogas to maintain the digester operating temperature at 38°C.

Finally, a compressed biogas pathway (CBG) to be used as a transportation fuel is modeled, which is analyzed separately as this pathway produces a different type of energy, precluding a direct compari- son with the electricity producing pathways. Never- theless, the CBG pathway is analyzed in this paper to highlight potential opportunities and disadvantages of diversifying the use of biogas, other than electricity production, as current transportation fuels are becoming supply limited. It is assumed that vehicle fuel quality compressed biogas is 96% CH4 content. The upgrading process demands 1.6 MJ of electricity per cubic meter of biogas and the compression process demands 0.6 MJ of electricity per cubic meter of biogas. During both of these processes, nearly 3.2% of the generated methane is lost through leakages in the system. 57 A summary of the calculated material and energy inputs for each unit-process and pathway analyzed in this study can be found in Table S4. Results and discussion Sustainability indicators below are expressed per GJ of energy produced. Net energy production is 20.3 GJ for the BC, AS, and INJ; 18.7 GJ for CTF; 27.7 GJ for BED; 25.2 GJ for CS; 28.7 GJ for SG; and 26.5 GJ for CHP. GHG emissions are presented in net terms, which considers that CO2(b) emitted throughout the stages of the AD pathways come from carbon uptake by corn stover, switchgrass, and the crops that consti- tute the dairy diet. Sustainability indicators of the base-case (BC) pathway Net GHG emissions for the BC are 243.3 kg CO2-eq GJ-1 of energy produced, which are analyzed accord- ing to the type of gas and the unit-process where GHG are emitted. When disaggregating these results according to the type of gas, 66% of the net GHG are emitted in the form of N2O(b) making it the primary contributor to this sustainability indicator, followed by 25% of net GHG emitted as CH4(b) and 9% emitted as CO2(f) from the combustion of diesel fuel. Nearly 41% of N2O(b) comes from indirect NH3 emissions through volatilization, which highlights the impor- tance of strategies or practices that reduce these emissions and their indirect contribution to climate change. As opposed to dairy operations without AD systems, N2O(b) emissions are higher than CH4(b)|emissions because the majority of degradable VS are destroyed in the digester significantly reducing CH4(b) emissions in downstream processes. Consequently, CH4(b) is mostly emitted in the biogas production and electricity generation unit-process due to losses and leakages. To better understand the source of these emissions, the data can be represented in terms of the unit-processes evaluated (Fig. 2(a)). Application of the liquid digestate fraction is the major contributor to GHG emissions, with N2O(t and CO2(f) being the most abundant gases in this unit-process. N2O(b) is a result of direct emissions through nitrification and denitrification processes in soils, and indirect emis- sions from ammonia volatilization. CO2(f) is emitted from the combustion of diesel fuel during land application and is higher than the rest of unit-pro- cesses due to the higher volumes of manure handled. For the BC, NH3 emissions are 17.1 kg NH3 GJ-1 of produced energy. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the majority of NH3 emissions come from storing liquid digestate. The conditions in storage do not promote the forma- tion of a natural crust given the low TS content of the liquid digestate that reaches storage. This is attributed to the VS destruction in the digester and the separa- tion of VS in the solid-liquid separation process. The inexistence of this crust promotes NH3 emissions on top of the stored digestate through aeration. NH3 emissions from liquid digestate during the land application unit-process are also significant because digestate is surface broadcast in the BC. The effect of injection in NH3 emissions is examined in the INJ pathway. Liquid digestate (application and storage) is responsible for most NH3 emissions as ammoniacal N remains primarily within the liquid stream after SLS. DFF is 306.8 MJ GJ-1 of produced energy and comes only from the consumption of diesel fuel given that all electricity and heat requirements are provided by the AD system. Liquid digestate land-application repre- sents the majority of this indicator due to the travel distances to spread manure and the volumes that are handled (Fig. 2(c)). Even though electricity and heat consumption do not contribute to DFF, they are estimated to determine the EROI ratio of the system. Electricity requirements for sand recovery, biogas production and cleaning, SLS, and digestate agitation during storage total 280 MJ GJ-1 and heat needed to maintain the digester at 38 °C is 939 MJ GJ-1 of produced energy. To calculate the EROI ratio, primary energy of 1062 MJ GJ-1 attributed to the addition of the digester is determined by subtracting the energy

Figure 2. (a) Contribution to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions of each unit-process in the base-case (BC) pathway; (b) Contribution to ammonia (NH3) emissions of each unit-process in the base-case (BC) pathway; (c) Contribution to depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) of each unit-process in the base-case (BC) pathway; (d) Nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassim (K) reaching the land in liquid and solid form after solid-liquid separation. Outer ring = total nutrient content; Inner ring: light color = nutrient in liquid fraction, dark color = nutrient in solid fraction.
consumption of a dairy system without a digester.18 Based on a total electricity production of 20.3 GJ-1 for the whole system, EROI is 0.94 and FER is 3.26 for the BC pathway.

N, P, and K are tracked to determine final nutrient availability. P and K are not affected by the unit-pro- cesses of the AD system, other than being partitioned between solid and liquid streams during SLS. Oppo- sitely, N is affected by losses as NH3 and N2O through- out the system and by changes in N form during the digestion process. For the BC, the majority of N, P,
and K content stays with the liquid stream after separation due to centrifugation efficiencies (Fig. 2(d)).

Oppo- sitely, N is affected by losses as NH3 and N2O through- out the system and by changes in N form during the digestion process. For the BC, the majority of N, P,
and K content stays with the liquid stream after separation due to centrifugation efficiencies (Fig. 2(d)).
19: This unit-process targets the transformation of biogas into its final energy form. The BC considers the production of electricity with an engine-generator-set with no heat recovery, given that the majority of AD systems installed in WI adopt this approach. 4 Two AD pathways are analyzed based on variations to this energy conversion unit-process. The first of these pathways (CHP) considers combined heat and power at 35% electricity efficiency and 50% thermal effi- ciency. CHP differentiates from BC by providing heat energy instead of combusting a fraction of the produced biogas to maintain the digester operating temperature at 38°C.

Finally, a compressed biogas pathway (CBG) to be used as a transportation fuel is modeled, which is analyzed separately as this pathway produces a different type of energy, precluding a direct compari- son with the electricity producing pathways. Never- theless, the CBG pathway is analyzed in this paper to highlight potential opportunities and disadvantages of diversifying the use of biogas, other than electricity production, as current transportation fuels are becoming supply limited. It is assumed that vehicle fuel quality compressed biogas is 96% CH4 content. The upgrading process demands 1.6 MJ of electricity per cubic meter of biogas and the compression process demands 0.6 MJ of electricity per cubic meter of biogas. During both of these processes, nearly 3.2% of the generated methane is lost through leakages in the system. 57 A summary of the calculated material and energy inputs for each unit-process and pathway analyzed in this study can be found in Table S4. Results and discussion Sustainability indicators below are expressed per GJ of energy produced. Net energy production is 20.3 GJ for the BC, AS, and INJ; 18.7 GJ for CTF; 27.7 GJ for BED; 25.2 GJ for CS; 28.7 GJ for SG; and 26.5 GJ for CHP. GHG emissions are presented in net terms, which considers that CO2(b) emitted throughout the stages of the AD pathways come from carbon uptake by corn stover, switchgrass, and the crops that consti- tute the dairy diet. Sustainability indicators of the base-case (BC) pathway Net GHG emissions for the BC are 243.3 kg CO2-eq GJ-1 of energy produced, which are analyzed accord- ing to the type of gas and the unit-process where GHG are emitted. When disaggregating these results according to the type of gas, 66% of the net GHG are emitted in the form of N2O(b) making it the primary contributor to this sustainability indicator, followed by 25% of net GHG emitted as CH4(b) and 9% emitted as CO2(f) from the combustion of diesel fuel. Nearly 41% of N2O(b) comes from indirect NH3 emissions through volatilization, which highlights the impor- tance of strategies or practices that reduce these emissions and their indirect contribution to climate change. As opposed to dairy operations without AD systems, N2O(b) emissions are higher than CH4(b)|emissions because the majority of degradable VS are destroyed in the digester significantly reducing CH4(b) emissions in downstream processes. Consequently, CH4(b) is mostly emitted in the biogas production and electricity generation unit-process due to losses and leakages. To better understand the source of these emissions, the data can be represented in terms of the unit-processes evaluated (Fig. 2(a)). Application of the liquid digestate fraction is the major contributor to GHG emissions, with N2O(t and CO2(f) being the most abundant gases in this unit-process. N2O(b) is a result of direct emissions through nitrification and denitrification processes in soils, and indirect emis- sions from ammonia volatilization. CO2(f) is emitted from the combustion of diesel fuel during land application and is higher than the rest of unit-pro- cesses due to the higher volumes of manure handled. For the BC, NH3 emissions are 17.1 kg NH3 GJ-1 of produced energy. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the majority of NH3 emissions come from storing liquid digestate. The conditions in storage do not promote the forma- tion of a natural crust given the low TS content of the liquid digestate that reaches storage. This is attributed to the VS destruction in the digester and the separa- tion of VS in the solid-liquid separation process. The inexistence of this crust promotes NH3 emissions on top of the stored digestate through aeration. NH3 emissions from liquid digestate during the land application unit-process are also significant because digestate is surface broadcast in the BC. The effect of injection in NH3 emissions is examined in the INJ pathway. Liquid digestate (application and storage) is responsible for most NH3 emissions as ammoniacal N remains primarily within the liquid stream after SLS. DFF is 306.8 MJ GJ-1 of produced energy and comes only from the consumption of diesel fuel given that all electricity and heat requirements are provided by the AD system. Liquid digestate land-application repre- sents the majority of this indicator due to the travel distances to spread manure and the volumes that are handled (Fig. 2(c)). Even though electricity and heat consumption do not contribute to DFF, they are estimated to determine the EROI ratio of the system. Electricity requirements for sand recovery, biogas production and cleaning, SLS, and digestate agitation during storage total 280 MJ GJ-1 and heat needed to maintain the digester at 38 °C is 939 MJ GJ-1 of produced energy. To calculate the EROI ratio, primary energy of 1062 MJ GJ-1 attributed to the addition of the digester is determined by subtracting the energy

Figure 2. (a) Contribution to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions of each unit-process in the base-case (BC) pathway; (b) Contribution to ammonia (NH3) emissions of each unit-process in the base-case (BC) pathway; (c) Contribution to depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) of each unit-process in the base-case (BC) pathway; (d) Nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassim (K) reaching the land in liquid and solid form after solid-liquid separation. Outer ring = total nutrient content; Inner ring: light color = nutrient in liquid fraction, dark color = nutrient in solid fraction.
consumption of a dairy system without a digester.18 Based on a total electricity production of 20.3 GJ-1 for the whole system, EROI is 0.94 and FER is 3.26 for the BC pathway.

N, P, and K are tracked to determine final nutrient availability. P and K are not affected by the unit-pro- cesses of the AD system, other than being partitioned between solid and liquid streams during SLS. Oppo- sitely, N is affected by losses as NH3 and N2O through- out the system and by changes in N form during the digestion process. For the BC, the majority of N, P,
and K content stays with the liquid stream after separation due to centrifugation efficiencies (Fig. 2(d)).
20: <header>Changes in sustainability indicators among AD pathways </header>

Sustainability indicators for each unit-process of the analyzed AD pathways are presented in Figs 3-6 and Table 3. Net GHG emissions range from 178 to 267 kg CO2-eq GJ-1. Even though this range is comparable to WI electricity grid emissions (234 kg CO2-eq GJ-1), studies showed that the installation of an AD system
Figure 3. Contribution to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from each unit-process and AD pathway.
Figure 4. Contribution to ammonia (NH3) emissions from each unit-process and AD pathway.

can reduce GHG emissions of manure management on a dairy farm by more than 40% when compared to a system without a digester, which highlights the benefits of AD systems in terms of net emissions reduction potential. 18 BED and CHP have the most significant decrease in GHG emissions when com- pared to the BC with 27%, and 24% reductions respectively. Given that the FU is defined as produced

energy, these reductions are mostly attributed to the higher energy efficiency of CHP, and the higher biogas production in BED (due to a higher availability of degradable VS from the digestate solids). BED also replaces sand with digested solids for cow bedding, which avoids the production and transportation of this resource. CS reduces GHG emissions by 3.8% despite the increase of emissions due to corn stover
Figure 5. Contribution to depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) from each unit-process and AD pathway.
Figure 6. Nutrient availability of the solid and liquid digestate fractions that reach the soil for each AD pathway.
Table 3. Total GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, DFF, N availability, EROI, and FER for the eight AD

collection, grinding, and the additional purchased fertilizer needed to replace N lost through volatiliza- tion. This reduction reflects the higher energy pro- duction rates obtained when co-digesting manure with corn stover. On the other hand, SG does not reduce GHG emissions given the increase in CH4(b) emissions from liquid digestate, which are a result of the additional VS from switchgrass that enter the storage. Recent literature shows that removal of corn stover for bioenergy production can not only reduce soil organic carbon but increase net GHG emissions because C in bioenergy is more rapidly oxidized than when added to soils. The removal rate of corn stover for AD would be different than the rate for cellulosic ethanol production if digestate is re-applied back to the land after biogas production. As this paper assumes re-application of digestate, the net removed C can be estimated by subtracting the C contained in biogas in the forms of CH4 and CO2 from the total C contained in removed corn stover. A net removal rate of 1 Mg Ha-1 yr was determined according to biogas production and composition, corn stover removal rates, and carbon content. Based on values for oxida- tion of soil organic carbon from residue removal presented in Liska et al., it was determined that an additional 29 kg CO2-eq GJ-1 of electricity should be attributed to the CS pathway due to emissions from C removal.5 58 These additional emissions would increase net GHG emissions of the CS pathway by 8% when compared to the BC. Further research is needed in order to establish a conclusive statement about GHG emissions related to C removal from corn stover used in anaerobic digestion systems given that there are other factors that have to be taken into account (e.g. how much C is actually re-applied to the soils, both from corn stover digestate and manure digestate). INJ reduces GHG emissions by 3.1% despite the increase in both N2O(b) emissions and the emissions from increased fossil fuel requirements. N2O(b) emissions are increased due to higher ammoniacal N reaching the soil as N2O(b) emissions depend on ammoniacal N. Fossil emissions from the combustion of diesel fuel are also increased because digestate injection is more energy intense than surface broad- cast methods. Net GHG emissions are reduced during this pathway as indirect N2O(b) from NH3 volatiliza- tion are also reduced. Moreover, there is more avail- able N for crop production which decreases the need to purchased N fertilizer. The AS pathway also|decreases GHG emissions during manure collection as diesel fuel is replaced by electricity produced by the AD system. CTF is the only pathway that in- creases GHG emissions by 9.7%. The first source of this increase comes from higher electricity require- ments of the centrifuge when compared to the screw press, which reduces overall electricity production. Second, the higher N concentrations in the solid stream increase N2O(b) emissions given that the EF for solid digestate is higher than the EF for liquid digestate. NH3 emissions range from 11 to 18 kg GJ-1. All AD pathways are effective in reducing NH3 emissions with the exception of CTF. Reducing these emissions through alternative practices is very important for AD systems given that previous studies have shown that the digestion process increases NH3 emissions when compared to a system without AD. 18 INJ reduces NH3 emissions by 37% due to the injection of both the liquid and solid digestate during the land-application unit-process and due to the avoided purchased fertilizer. Decreases of NH3 emissions from the remaining pathways are attributed to higher biogas production rates. The increase of NH3 emissions in CTF comes from both storage and land-application of solid digestate given that more ammoniacal N is separated along with this fraction by centrifugation. Previous studies have concluded that the installation of an anaerobic digester can reduce up to 48% of GHG emissions per ton of manure processes, but increase NH3 emissions by nearly 40% in a dairy farm without a digester. If the results of this paper were expressed per ton of material being processed, results would show that management practices are able to further reduce GHG emissions by another 5% after the digester installation. On the other hand, injection alone would reduce NH3 emissions by nearly 40%, highlighting the importance of management practices to diminish the negative impacts of AD systems in terms of ammonia emissions. Figure 7 shows the relation and trade-offs between GHG and NH3 emissions among pathways. BED is the pathway with the highest reduction of both GHG and NH3 emis- sions per unit of energy produced followed by CHP. The pathway INJ is the most effective in reducing NH3 emissions, but is not as effective in reducing GHG emissions. This same trend happens with biomass co-digestion pathways. SG brings down NH3 emissions when compared to CS, but CS is more effective in reducing GHG emissions than SG due to

Figure 7. Comparison of ammonia (NH3) and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from the different pathways analyzed: BC = base-case, INJ = digestate injection, BED = digested solids used as bedding, CHP = combined heat and power, SG = switchgrass co-digestion, CS = corn stover co-digestion, AS = alley scraper to collect manure, CTF = centrifuge for solid-liquid separation.
the higher VS content of switchgrass digestate enter- ing the storage. Finally, CTF increases both NH3 and GHG emissions when compared to the BC due to the lower net electricity production.

DFF ranges from 176 to 327 MJ GJ-1, which is a significant improvement from the WI electricity GRID rate of 3,500 MJ GJ . INJ and BED decrease DFF the most with 42% and 31% reductions. These reductions come from the avoided production of N fertilizer (urea) and sand bedding respectively, which shows the significant amounts of fossil energy in- volved in the manufacturing of these resources. AS also reduces DFF as it replaces the use of diesel during manure collection with electricity generated by the AD system. Oppositely, CTF and CS increase DFF by 6.6%, and 6.2%, respectively. DFF is increased in CTF due to higher electricity requirements for the centrifu- gation process which reduces overall energy produced by the system. DFF is also increased in CS due to higher N fertilizer and diesel requirements. EROI ratios are increased by all pathways except for the CTF pathway that reflects a 13% reduction. CHP increases EROI by 36%, showing the effectiveness of recovering heat from the electricity generation process.
N availability ranges from 7 to 15 kg GJ-1 of pro- duced energy depending on the AD pathway. Given

that the FU is produced energy, nutrient availability among pathways changes according to net energy production. Prior to dividing the sustainability indicators by the functional unit, P and K availability for the entire system present no change among AD pathways, with the exception of the CTF and BED pathways, which have higher separation efficiencies and recycle solids back into the system respectively. N availability is affected mostly by losses through volatilization. As a result, N availability is increased in the INJ pathway by 42%.

collection, grinding, and the additional purchased fertilizer needed to replace N lost through volatiliza- tion. This reduction reflects the higher energy pro- duction rates obtained when co-digesting manure with corn stover. On the other hand, SG does not reduce GHG emissions given the increase in CH4(b) emissions from liquid digestate, which are a result of the additional VS from switchgrass that enter the storage. Recent literature shows that removal of corn stover for bioenergy production can not only reduce soil organic carbon but increase net GHG emissions because C in bioenergy is more rapidly oxidized than when added to soils. The removal rate of corn stover for AD would be different than the rate for cellulosic ethanol production if digestate is re-applied back to the land after biogas production. As this paper assumes re-application of digestate, the net removed C can be estimated by subtracting the C contained in biogas in the forms of CH4 and CO2 from the total C contained in removed corn stover. A net removal rate of 1 Mg Ha-1 yr was determined according to biogas production and composition, corn stover removal rates, and carbon content. Based on values for oxida- tion of soil organic carbon from residue removal presented in Liska et al., it was determined that an additional 29 kg CO2-eq GJ-1 of electricity should be attributed to the CS pathway due to emissions from C removal.5 58 These additional emissions would increase net GHG emissions of the CS pathway by 8% when compared to the BC. Further research is needed in order to establish a conclusive statement about GHG emissions related to C removal from corn stover used in anaerobic digestion systems given that there are other factors that have to be taken into account (e.g. how much C is actually re-applied to the soils, both from corn stover digestate and manure digestate). INJ reduces GHG emissions by 3.1% despite the increase in both N2O(b) emissions and the emissions from increased fossil fuel requirements. N2O(b) emissions are increased due to higher ammoniacal N reaching the soil as N2O(b) emissions depend on ammoniacal N. Fossil emissions from the combustion of diesel fuel are also increased because digestate injection is more energy intense than surface broad- cast methods. Net GHG emissions are reduced during this pathway as indirect N2O(b) from NH3 volatiliza- tion are also reduced. Moreover, there is more avail- able N for crop production which decreases the need to purchased N fertilizer. The AS pathway also|decreases GHG emissions during manure collection as diesel fuel is replaced by electricity produced by the AD system. CTF is the only pathway that in- creases GHG emissions by 9.7%. The first source of this increase comes from higher electricity require- ments of the centrifuge when compared to the screw press, which reduces overall electricity production. Second, the higher N concentrations in the solid stream increase N2O(b) emissions given that the EF for solid digestate is higher than the EF for liquid digestate. NH3 emissions range from 11 to 18 kg GJ-1. All AD pathways are effective in reducing NH3 emissions with the exception of CTF. Reducing these emissions through alternative practices is very important for AD systems given that previous studies have shown that the digestion process increases NH3 emissions when compared to a system without AD. 18 INJ reduces NH3 emissions by 37% due to the injection of both the liquid and solid digestate during the land-application unit-process and due to the avoided purchased fertilizer. Decreases of NH3 emissions from the remaining pathways are attributed to higher biogas production rates. The increase of NH3 emissions in CTF comes from both storage and land-application of solid digestate given that more ammoniacal N is separated along with this fraction by centrifugation. Previous studies have concluded that the installation of an anaerobic digester can reduce up to 48% of GHG emissions per ton of manure processes, but increase NH3 emissions by nearly 40% in a dairy farm without a digester. If the results of this paper were expressed per ton of material being processed, results would show that management practices are able to further reduce GHG emissions by another 5% after the digester installation. On the other hand, injection alone would reduce NH3 emissions by nearly 40%, highlighting the importance of management practices to diminish the negative impacts of AD systems in terms of ammonia emissions. Figure 7 shows the relation and trade-offs between GHG and NH3 emissions among pathways. BED is the pathway with the highest reduction of both GHG and NH3 emis- sions per unit of energy produced followed by CHP. The pathway INJ is the most effective in reducing NH3 emissions, but is not as effective in reducing GHG emissions. This same trend happens with biomass co-digestion pathways. SG brings down NH3 emissions when compared to CS, but CS is more effective in reducing GHG emissions than SG due to

Figure 7. Comparison of ammonia (NH3) and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from the different pathways analyzed: BC = base-case, INJ = digestate injection, BED = digested solids used as bedding, CHP = combined heat and power, SG = switchgrass co-digestion, CS = corn stover co-digestion, AS = alley scraper to collect manure, CTF = centrifuge for solid-liquid separation.
the higher VS content of switchgrass digestate enter- ing the storage. Finally, CTF increases both NH3 and GHG emissions when compared to the BC due to the lower net electricity production.

DFF ranges from 176 to 327 MJ GJ-1, which is a significant improvement from the WI electricity GRID rate of 3,500 MJ GJ . INJ and BED decrease DFF the most with 42% and 31% reductions. These reductions come from the avoided production of N fertilizer (urea) and sand bedding respectively, which shows the significant amounts of fossil energy in- volved in the manufacturing of these resources. AS also reduces DFF as it replaces the use of diesel during manure collection with electricity generated by the AD system. Oppositely, CTF and CS increase DFF by 6.6%, and 6.2%, respectively. DFF is increased in CTF due to higher electricity requirements for the centrifu- gation process which reduces overall energy produced by the system. DFF is also increased in CS due to higher N fertilizer and diesel requirements. EROI ratios are increased by all pathways except for the CTF pathway that reflects a 13% reduction. CHP increases EROI by 36%, showing the effectiveness of recovering heat from the electricity generation process.
N availability ranges from 7 to 15 kg GJ-1 of pro- duced energy depending on the AD pathway. Given

that the FU is produced energy, nutrient availability among pathways changes according to net energy production. Prior to dividing the sustainability indicators by the functional unit, P and K availability for the entire system present no change among AD pathways, with the exception of the CTF and BED pathways, which have higher separation efficiencies and recycle solids back into the system respectively. N availability is affected mostly by losses through volatilization. As a result, N availability is increased in the INJ pathway by 42%.

26: Net energy production for the CBG pathway is 82.3 GJ. In terms of GHG and NH3 emissions, CBG emits 100 kg CO2-eq GJ-1 and 4.2 kg NH3 GJ- of energy produced respectively. These results are significantly lower than the emissions from the BC given the higher energy efficiency of this conversion process when compared to electricity production. The analysis points out however, that there are higher methane leakages during biogas compression and upgrading to transportation fuel quality. In terms of DFF, results are 566 MJ GJ-1 energy produced for the CBG path- way. DFF is significantly increased given that all electricity requirements of the system are provided

with electricity from the WI grid, which has nearly 75% of its mix coming from fossil sources (i.e., coal and natural gas). Moreover, the CBG pathway shows a 1.4 EROI, which is influenced by the energy intensity involved in producing WI grid electricity.

Comparison of these results with those of other studies should be done with caution as assumptions and model parameters may be different. For example, the GREET model is currently used by the RFS2 and the California Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) to evaluate the lifecycle impacts of transportation fuels. 1,59 As other federal and state regulations for GHG emissions are implemented, it is likely that second generation biofuels, such as biogas, become more demanded by the transportation sector and that the GREET model will continue to be the preferred method to evaluate their environmental impacts. As this process develops, it is important that relevant parameters of studies such as this one are incorpo- rated into the GREET model in order to be recog- nized by state and federal regulations.
