28: Assessing the environmental impacts of different AD systems cannot be conducted with a one size fits all approach as the selected pathways have a substantial influence on results. GHG emissions can be reduced 31% by management practices alone, 24% if heat from the electricity generation process is recovered, and 4% by co-digesting manure with corn stover. Results also show that injection is an effective management practice to reduce NH3 emissions, and thus, to increase N availability of the digestate that reaches the soil after the digestion process. The increase in N availability has environmental benefits not only for GHG emissions due to avoided urea production and application, but also for DFF as manufacturing of fertilizer is very intensive in fossil energy resources. Replacing diesel fuel with electricity produced by the AD system promotes GHG emission reductions as manure is collected with an alley scraper instead of a skid-steer. Separating digestate through centrifugation is effective in concentrating P, K, and N in the solid stream, but is also more energy demanding than a screw press. Replacing sand with digested solids for cow bedding contributes to reduce DFF, NH3 emis- sions, and GHG emissions.
In terms of co-digestion strategies, results show that mixing manure with biomass feedstocks increases

energy production. Co-digesting corn stover with manure is an effective strategy to reduce GHG emis- sions as this feedstock requires harvesting as opposed to switchgrass that needs to be added to the already existing crop mix requiring additional planting as well as harvesting. On the other hand, SG is more effective to increase EROI and decrease DFF and NH3 emissions than CS due to higher biogas production yields achieved with this feedstock. Overall, all AD pathways increase EROI when compared to the BC except CTF as this pathway demands more energy than BC. Finally, results show that heating the di- gester with recovered heat from the generator im- proves all sustainability indicators, highlighting the potential of this pathway to maximize energy effi- ciency and reduce environmental impacts without adding major technical or economic challenges to the farmer.

This analysis pinpoints the potential that anaerobic digestion has as a sustainable energy generation technology, but there are opportunities for future work. The SG pathway assumes that there is available land to grow switchgrass, which might not be the case for the majority of dairy farms in WI. In addition, there is remaining carbon stored in switchgrass roots that was not considered in this study and that could affect net GHG emissions results. It is also assumed that the digestion system is flexible to add extra volume in the co-digestion scenarios, which might pose technical and economical limitations for the farmer. There may also be some synergistic effects when combining pathways that change management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conver- sion process. The effect of management practices alone could be modeled to evaluate their effect on sustain- ability indicators versus the installation of an anaero- bic digester. Additional pathways that explore man- agement practices of liquid digestate storage are needed as this unit-process is a major contributor to GHG and NH3 emissions. Moreover, there are other readily available waste streams in WI (e.g. cheese whey) that could be included in the digester as biomass feedstocks.

WI internal energy supply is currently limited by the scarcity of fossil fuel reserves. The state has a clear competitive advantage for biogas production due to the availability and reliability of biomass resources, but there are some barriers that limit the widespread adoption of AD systems. Among some of the barriers are high initial investment, little information about

ways to maximize profitability (i.e., co-digestion), lack of knowledge about environmental benefits, and inexistence of specific regulations. The state of Cali- fornia has been leading research that could guide strategies to overcome these barriers. The California State Water Resources Control Board put together a review of technologies, markets and regulations of dairy digesters for biogas production. 60 The study found some of the factors driving profitability of AD systems are energy prices, legislative support, involve- ment of utilities, access to capital third party develop- ers, air quality regulation, and proximity to feedstocks and energy infrastructure. Sanchez examined the financial and policy barriers of biogas development in California and found that revenues are more sensitive to carbon offset prices than electricity prices. 61 The recommendations of the author are to develop a robust carbon offset market, implement co-digestion where feasible, and continue promoting information exchange.

The results of this study seek to provide useful information to farmers, researchers and policy makers in the process of overcoming existing barriers for AD implementation. The range of biogas pathways could help current dairy farmers with AD systems to adopt new strategies or help new adopters to define specific pathways according to their priorities and needs. Moreover, the analyzed AD pathways could be included in existing policy frameworks or provide guidelines in the process of developing new climate and energy policies that adjust to the versatility of biogas at regional and national levels. This paper also seeks to provide a methodological framework when conducting AD LCA studies and serve as a reference point when including the agricultural phase of residues in the analysis.

On the other hand, SG is more effective to increase EROI and decrease DFF and NH3 emissions than CS due to higher biogas production yields achieved with this feedstock. Overall, all AD pathways increase EROI when compared to the BC except CTF as this pathway demands more energy than BC. Finally, results show that heating the di- gester with recovered heat from the generator im- proves all sustainability indicators, highlighting the potential of this pathway to maximize energy effi- ciency and reduce environmental impacts without adding major technical or economic challenges to the farmer.

This analysis pinpoints the potential that anaerobic digestion has as a sustainable energy generation technology, but there are opportunities for future work. The SG pathway assumes that there is available land to grow switchgrass, which might not be the case for the majority of dairy farms in WI. In addition, there is remaining carbon stored in switchgrass roots that was not considered in this study and that could affect net GHG emissions results. It is also assumed that the digestion system is flexible to add extra volume in the co-digestion scenarios, which might pose technical and economical limitations for the farmer. There may also be some synergistic effects when combining pathways that change management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conver- sion process. The effect of management practices alone could be modeled to evaluate their effect on sustain- ability indicators versus the installation of an anaero- bic digester. Additional pathways that explore man- agement practices of liquid digestate storage are needed as this unit-process is a major contributor to GHG and NH3 emissions. Moreover, there are other readily available waste streams in WI (e.g. cheese whey) that could be included in the digester as biomass feedstocks.

WI internal energy supply is currently limited by the scarcity of fossil fuel reserves. The state has a clear competitive advantage for biogas production due to the availability and reliability of biomass resources, but there are some barriers that limit the widespread adoption of AD systems. Among some of the barriers are high initial investment, little information about

ways to maximize profitability (i.e., co-digestion), lack of knowledge about environmental benefits, and inexistence of specific regulations. The state of Cali- fornia has been leading research that could guide strategies to overcome these barriers. The California State Water Resources Control Board put together a review of technologies, markets and regulations of dairy digesters for biogas production. 60 The study found some of the factors driving profitability of AD systems are energy prices, legislative support, involve- ment of utilities, access to capital third party develop- ers, air quality regulation, and proximity to feedstocks and energy infrastructure. Sanchez examined the financial and policy barriers of biogas development in California and found that revenues are more sensitive to carbon offset prices than electricity prices. 61 The recommendations of the author are to develop a robust carbon offset market, implement co-digestion where feasible, and continue promoting information exchange.

The results of this study seek to provide useful information to farmers, researchers and policy makers in the process of overcoming existing barriers for AD implementation. The range of biogas pathways could help current dairy farmers with AD systems to adopt new strategies or help new adopters to define specific pathways according to their priorities and needs. Moreover, the analyzed AD pathways could be included in existing policy frameworks or provide guidelines in the process of developing new climate and energy policies that adjust to the versatility of biogas at regional and national levels. This paper also seeks to provide a methodological framework when conducting AD LCA studies and serve as a reference point when including the agricultural phase of residues in the analysis.
30: Assessing the environmental impacts of different AD systems cannot be conducted with a one size fits all approach as the selected pathways have a substantial influence on results. GHG emissions can be reduced 31% by management practices alone, 24% if heat from the electricity generation process is recovered, and 4% by co-digesting manure with corn stover. Results also show that injection is an effective management practice to reduce NH3 emissions, and thus, to increase N availability of the digestate that reaches the soil after the digestion process. The increase in N availability has environmental benefits not only for GHG emissions due to avoided urea production and application, but also for DFF as manufacturing of fertilizer is very intensive in fossil energy resources. Replacing diesel fuel with electricity produced by the AD system promotes GHG emission reductions as manure is collected with an alley scraper instead of a skid-steer. Separating digestate through centrifugation is effective in concentrating P, K, and N in the solid stream, but is also more energy demanding than a screw press. Replacing sand with digested solids for cow bedding contributes to reduce DFF, NH3 emis- sions, and GHG emissions.
In terms of co-digestion strategies, results show that mixing manure with biomass feedstocks increases

energy production. Co-digesting corn stover with manure is an effective strategy to reduce GHG emis- sions as this feedstock requires harvesting as opposed to switchgrass that needs to be added to the already existing crop mix requiring additional planting as well as harvesting. 48: Horacio Aguirre-Villegas is a Post- doctoral Research Associate in the Biological Systems Engineer- ing Department at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. His research lies in the fields of bioenergy, climate change, waste management, and life cycle assessment (LCA). In particular, he focuses on evaluating the sustainability of agricultural and food pro- duction systems and their integration with bio-energy systems from a life cycle perspective, pinpointing areas of improvement.
49: Rebecca Larson is an assistant professor and extension specialist in the Biological Systems Engineering Department at UW-Madison focus- ing on biological waste issues. Becky completed her BSc, MSc, and PhD in Biosystems Engineering Department at Michigan State University. Her research and extension interests include all areas of biological waste including manure manage- ment, handling and treatment of agricultural waste, diffuse source pollution, agricultural sustainability, and waste-to-energy technologies including biogas produc- tion from anaerobic digestion.
50: Douglas J. Reinemann is a profes- sor and Chairman of the Biological Systems Engineering Department at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. His research and educational inter- ests include energy use and energy production in agricultural systems. He is a member of the sustainability group of the UW Great Lakes Bioenergy Research Center examining environmental impacts of biofuels produc- tion systems. He also leads the UW 'green cheese' team who is investigating synergies between dairy and biofuels production systems in Wisconsin and has been actively involved with the Midwest Rural Energy Council.
